text: The surgical team demonstrates port placement for the esophagectomy, ensuring ports are positioned at least ten centimeters flat to the umbilicus to aid mediastinal dissection. The peritoneal cavity and hepatic lobes are inspected, revealing no gross metastases. An incarcerated umbilical hernia is identified, reduced, and repaired.

The surgeon places a liver retractor and opens the thickened pars flaccida, which is visibly affected by radiation therapy. The dissection proceeds laterally towards the caudate lobe and continues towards the frontal esophageal ligament, with the thickened tissue posing a challenge due to prior neoadjuvant therapy.

The surgeon creates a retroesophageal window and passes a Penrose drain to manipulate the gastroesophageal junction and pull down the esophagus specimen during mediastinal dissection. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to visualize the right gastroepiploic vessel and its branches, which are crucial for the gastric conduit. The Firefly system is employed for enhanced three-dimensional visualization during dissection, alternating with regular vision.

The surgeon mixes 25 milligrams of ICG Green with 10 cc's of saline and administers 1.5 cc's to enhance visualization of the vasculature. The posterior wall of the stomach is grasped to avoid the vascular pedicle, ensuring the preservation of the superior arcade. Edema is visible in the tissue, likely due to prior radiation therapy.

The surgeon focuses on cocorizing the duodenum, with the gastrocolic pedicle visible to the right. The groove between the duodenum and colon begins to develop, aided by blunt dissection and a vessel sealer. ICG Green is used to confirm perfusion of the gastric conduit, with vessels visibly illuminated superiorly.

The surgeon skeletonizes the left gastric pedicle to its base, using a white load stapler to transect both the artery and vein simultaneously. This step is crucial for adequate lymphadenectomy due to the presence of an avid lymph node around the gastric pedicle. Following this, the transhiatal esophageal dissection is continued, initially challenging due to the radiated field, but becomes easier as the dissection progresses cephalad into the posterior mediastinum. The hiatus is snug, prompting the surgeon to open it by transecting part of the right crus.

The surgeon performs ICG green perfusion to confirm that the right gastroepiploic vessel remains intact, followed by the application of the final staple line using green and blue loads. The penrose drain is removed, and the specimen is pulled from the neck as the dissection is completed. The gastric conduit is sutured to the specimen and carefully guided through the mediastinum to prevent twisting on the vascular pedicle. The conduit is pulled without tension, ensuring proper orientation. A post-operative upper GI study shows good flow with no evidence of leakage in the neck, and there is smooth transit into the duodenum without pooling.
